s~fficiently constant new form, Lychnis diurna glabra alba'. But in the 1899 paper he 
offered no explanation for the different patterns of inheritance in the progeny of F2 
plants with red and those with white flowers. The absence of such an explanatio~ 
suggests to me that as late as 1899 he had not yet differentiated the two kinds of 
dominants, contrary to his own claim that by 1896 he had discovered the 1:2:1 ratio. 
His description of the recurrence of about 6% plants with white flowers in the F3, F4, 
and F 5 generations bespeaks bafflement, rather than understanding. 
2.3. de Vries's 1903 account. De Vries had stated, in 1897, that among the 600 F z 
plants from his Linaria cross, there were about 80~ 
with orange flowers and about 
20% with yellow. He did not refer to this cross in the 1900 rediscovery papersl but he 
did describe it in full in volume two of Die Mutationstheorie (1903). Here he gave the 
raw data for the F 2 generation: 135 of 617 plants, or 21'9~o, had pale yellow 
flowers, z~ The Linaria situation in 1897 now seems clear, too. Presumably in 1897 de 
Vries possessed the same raw data of 482:135 (78"1 ~ :21"9% ) as reported in 1903; 
but in 1897 he saw in it only a 80~ : 20% ratio, the closer of the two simple ratios 4 : 1 
and 3:1. In contrast, in 1903 he interpreted these data as a reasonably close 
approximation to the expected Mendelian 250/0 . The same argument made above for 
the Lychnis experiment is applicable here, but it is strengthened because of the 
'better' Linaria data. If de Vries had been aware ofa 3 : 1 ratio by 1896, why didn't he 
regard the 21"9% plants with yellow flowers as the 25% recessive? It is worth noting 
that the Papaver data from ] 895, which he cited as the basis for his discovery of the 
3:1 ratio (see section 5), was hardly any 'better': 158 to 43 (22"5% recessive) (in the 
Appendix, see A.5 of sub-section 6.1). 
There seems to be a third account of the Lychnis cross, also in volume two of Die 
Mutationstheorie, in de Vries's discussion of his dihybrid crosses. As already 
mentioned, in 1900 in the rediscovery papers he included data for flower colour from 
the cross Lychnis diurna x Lychnis vespertina glabra as evidence for a 3 : 1 ratio. In 
1903 he gave the details of this cross. The second generation of hybrids (1894) was 
composed of I 16 red-flowered plants and 42 white-flowered ones. z 1 In the section on 
dihybrid crosses he discussed the simultaneous inheritance of hairiness and flower 
colour in Lychnis. Citing a Lychnis diurna x Lychnis vespertina glabra cross begun in 
1892, he reported for the F z generation 70 plants with hair and red flowers, 23 with 
hair and white flowers, 46 without hair and with red flowers, and 19 without hair and 
with white flowers (in the Appendix, see sub-section 6.3). De Vries recognized in 1903 
the marked deviation fi'om the expected 9 : 3 : 3 : 1 ratio, attributing it to the small 
size of the sample. 2z The experimental result is relevant once one decomposes the 
dihybrid F 2 ratio into its two monohybrid F z ratios. For flower colour, one obtains 
70+ 46 = 116 red flowers and 23 + 19 =42 white flowers. Thus the 116:42 ratio for 
flower colour, separately reported in 1903, clearly came from this dihybrid cross. For

hairiness, one obtains 70 % 23 = 93 hairy plants and 46 + 19 = 65 hairless plants. This

Section II ('Die typischen Bastardspaltungen') of Part II ('Die Mendel'schen 
Spaltungsgesetze') of volume two of Die Mutationstheorie contains the fullest 
account of de Vries's Mendelian experiments 
from the 
1890s. When 
Die 
Mutationstheorie was translated into English (translation published in 1909-1910), 
'some chapters, especially among those on hybridization, which seem to be of too 
technical a nature for the general student... [were] omitted from the second 
volume'. 23 A separate translation of these chapters was contemplated, but it never 
appeared. In fact the material omitted from volume two exceeds in length the 
material included. Among the omitted sections is section II Of part II mentioned 
above. For this reason I have included in the Appendix a near-complete account of 
these experiments. It is worth remarking that there are some discrepancies, 
involving data and dates, between de Vries's 1900 and 1903 accounts of his 
experiments. Most of the monohybrid (3 : l) data are identical in 1900 and 1903, but 
there are the following changes (1900 ~ recessive, 1903 ~ recessive, reference to sub- 
section 6.1 of the Appendix): Papaver somnif. Mephisto x Danebrog (280/0, 22"50/0, 
A.5), 24 Aster tripolium x album (270/0,250/0, B.I ), Chrysanthemum roxburghi x album 
(23~Yo, 2()~ 
B.2), Solanum nigrum • chlorocarpum (24%, 23%, C.2) and Lychnis 
vespertina glabra x L. vespertina (28~Yo, 27~o, D.3). In 1900 de Vries reported one 
1:2:1 FE ratio (24% :51%:25%) and stated that the F z heterozygotes when crossed 
gave, in the F3, 77% dominant and 23% recessive. 25 The 1903 account is different on 
both counts. As just indicated, de Vries obtained 77-5% dominant in the F 2. He 
tested 13 F2 dominants and found 4 were true breeding; thus 4/13 x 77"5% = 24% 
were Mephisto in the F2 and 9/13 • 77'5% = 53"5% were hybrids. Finally 22"5% were 
Danebrog, so the 1903 ratio would be 24% :53"5% : 22"5%. 26 In the 1903 account, the 
9 heterozygous F 2 plants produced, in the Fa, 1095 dominant and 358 recessive 
offspring or 75"5% and 24"5% (compare the 1900 figures of 77% and 23%; and in the 
Appendix see A.5 of sub-section 6.1). Lastly, for the back-cross experiments, the % 
recessive changed from 50% (1900) to 49% (1903) for Clarkia pulcheUa XX white 
(Appendix sub-section 6.2, 3) and likewise for Silene armeria XX white (Appendix 
sub-section 6.2, 6). 
De Vries, in 1900, listed the 'Year of crossing' for his artificial crosses, the 'Year of 
experiment ~ for the free crosses and for the back-crosses and elsewhere in the text of 
the German paper gave dates for the different generations in two experiments. The 
fuller 1903 accounts gavd dates for almost all experiments. When dates in the two 
cases are compared, they reveal some discrepancies. If one takes the fuller 1903 
accounts as correct,, then his 1900 date represented, in five instances, the year of the 
cross (P x P), as stated; in seven instances the F~ generation; in six instances the F 2 
generation; and in one instance neither P, F1, nor F z.

3. Xenia and de Vries's discovery of a 3:1 F 2 ratio 
Until the very end of the nineteenth century, botanists believed that in plant 
reproduction the traits of the seed itself were determined by the ovule-producing 
plant (mother) only. The influence of the pollen-providing plant (father) was first 
observable in the plant grown from the seed. However, a number of cases were known 
in which the seed exhibited the seed traits of the pollen plant. That is, there appeared 
to be a direct effect of the foreign pollen; the sign of a cross was visible immediately, 
rather than in the next generation. Maize or corn (Zea) provided many of the best 
examples of this phenomenon which W. Focke named 'xenia' in his treatise Die 
Pflanzen-Mischlinffe (1881 ). 28 Both de Vries and Correns undertook investigations of 
xenia in the 1890s. 29 
De Vries recounted, in 1900, how he had begun experiments on the direct 
influence of pollen in maize in 1876. He grew sweet corn (kernels with a sugary 
endosperm) next to ordinary corn (kernels with a starchy endosperm) and left cross- 
fertilization to the wind. He found a direct influence of starchy corn on sweet corn, 
but not vice versa. He also grew black corn next to white corn and found a direct 
influence of black on white. He continued to grow corn plants after 1876, but with 
many interruptions because of the unfavourable climate. In bad years, no cars 
ripened. 3~ 
De Vries returned to the study of xenia by means of hybridization in 1898. He 
crossed a variety of starchy corn with a variety of sweet corn (see Figures 1 and 2). 
Simultaneous self-fertilization experiments in 1898 and 1899 established the 
constancy of the two varieties used in 1898 for hybridization. De Vries removed most 
of the tassels (~) from the sweet corn before flowering. When the silks (~) were ready 
for fertilization de Vries applied pollen from the starchy corn, 'but without 
preventing completely fertilization by pollen coming from lower branches of their 
own [that is, the sweet corn's] male inflorescences', al The cross produced ten full 
ears, each one of which bore both starchy and sweet kernels, a2 On some ears most of 
the kernels were starchy, while on others most ('les trois quarts ou les deux tiers') 
were sweet. The starchy kernels 
cxhibiting the direct effect of the pollen--were the 
product of hybridization, while the sweet kernels were evidently the product of self- 
fertilization. 
De Vries was able to prove this suspected origin of the sweet kernels the next year 
when all gave rise to plants which, when self-fertilized, yielded sweet kernels only. De 
Vries's decision to leave some tassels on the sweet corn and thus permit some self- 
fertilization had a purpose. He noted that he could have removed completely the 
tassels from the sweet corn. But in that case, after the cross, the ears would have had 
starchy kernels only and thus would have been indistinguishable from the ears on a 
self-fertilized starchy plant. The hybrid origin of the ear would not have been visibly 
obvious. But de Vries's experimental design produced mixed ears on sweet corn

kernels had starchy endosperms, while wrinkled kernels had sweet endosperms.

plants, the starchy kernels of which were clearly the result of hybridization. 33 He 
chose one of the most beautiful ears as the seed source for the next generation of 1899. 
This ear had 180 starchy kernels and 66 sweet ones. These two numbers 180 and 66.

These numbers had nothing to do with an F2 generation. The 180 starchy kernels 
were the F 1 hybrids, while the 66 sweet kernels were not hybrids at all, but rather 
were the result of (hoped-for) self-fertilization. 34 
De Vries separated the two kinds of kernels and planted some of each kind. As

Hugo de Vries and the Rediscovery of Mendel's Laws 
527 
De Vries published two papers on xenia, both written in French and both 
submitted for publication in December 1899. 36 There is a perfect parallel between 
these two xenia papers and his two French rediscovery papers of 1900. The shorter 
xenia paper and the shorter rediscovery paper (the first of the rediscovery papers to 
appear in print) were submitted for publication in the Comptes rendus de l'Acad~mie 
des Sciences in Paris. Both appeared within a short time (about one month after 
submission). The longer papers on xenia and the rediscovery were submitted for 
publication in the Revue g~n~rale de botanique at the same time as the respective 
shorter papers were submitted to the Acad~mie des Sciences. Each appeared after 
four to five months. 
Since the shorter xenia paper appeared in December 1899, it contains de Vries's 
first published 3 : 1 F 2 ratio. And if, in fact, he did not report a 3 : 1 F 2 ratio in July 1899 
at the International Conference, the xenia paper would also be his first presentation 
of such a ratio to the scientific world. Zirkle argued that de Vries did not know the 3 : 1 
ratio (in any case) in July 1899, that he learned it from Mendel's paper which he first 
read sometime after July 1899, and furthermore that he was led to Mendel's paper by 
a paper by Carl Correns on xenia. 
Correns began his own study of xenia by means of hybridization in 1894. In 1901, 
in the foreword to his monograph on maize hybridization, he stated that he had 
obtained most of his results by the fall of 1897. However, his analysis of these results 
was delayed for two years while he completed a massive monograph on asexual 
reproduction in mosses.37 In December 1899 he wrote a paper on xenia. The paper 
was submitted for publication a few days before de Vries's shorter xenia paper 
appeared; thus Correns's paper was independent of de Vries's. In his paper Correns 
discussed the F 1 generation only, merely alluding to crosses between hybrids: 'For 
the present, I will publish the experimentally obtained results so far as they relate to 
pure races; the hybrids between races present very interesting, but also very 
complicated relationships, into which I cannot enter here'. 3s Thus Correns did not 
give a 3 : l ratio, nor any other ratio, in his xenia paper. In the very last sentence of 
the paper he referred to both Darwin and Mendel. Correns wanted to point out that 
seed colour was sometimes due to the embryo, rather than the endosperm, so that 
change in seed colour after fertilization was not always a case of xenia, which 
involved the endosperm only. 'It is essentially the same phenomenon, as occurs in 
the hybridization of pea races with yellow and green seeds, which Darwin and Mendel 
already pointed out correctly'. 39 
Zirkle felt that de Vries was probably led to Mendel by Correns's reference, even 
though Correns did not give a citation (nor even a first name) for Mendel. There is no 
doubt, as Zirkle noted, that de Vries knew of Correns's xenia paper. In his longer 
xenia paper, de Vries referred to Correns's paper in a footnote. The reference to 
Correns must have been added by de Vries in proof, some time between 25 January 
36Dunn ((%Otllote 29), 111) mistakenly gave April 1900 and August 1900 as the submission and 
publication dates, respectively, fer the longer xenia paper. The correct dates are December 1899 and April 
1900. 
37C. Correns, 'Bastarde zwischen Maisrassen mit besonderer Berficksiehtigung der Xenien'. 
Bibliotheca botanica 53 (1901),v xii, 1-161 (p. v). 
38 C. Correns, 'Untersuchungen fiber die Xenien bei Zea Mays', Ber. Deutsch. Bot. Gesellsch., 17 (1899). 
410-417 (p. 411). By December 1899 Correns had discovered the Mendelian laws. In 1901 he wrote that the 
'remarkable regularities' that appeared in crosses of maize hybrids could be understood once he had fimnd 
similar, but much clearer, regularities in crosses of pea hybrids (C. Correns (footnote 37), v). 
39 C. Correns (tbotnote 38), 417.

528 
Malcolm J. Kottler 
and April 1900. 4~ But Zirkle overlooked completely de Vries's shorter xenia paper, 
which appeared before de Vries could have read Correns's paper. Since the two papers 
by de Vries are identical as far as F 2 data are concerned, we can conclude definitely 
that de Vries had obtained at least one 3 : 1 F 2 ratio before he read Correns's paper. It 
is still possible that de Vries found the 3 : 1 ratio only after reading Mendel's paper; 
but if this is so, it cannot have been Correns's paper on xenia that led de Vries to 
Mendel. It may have been Correns's paper, however, that informed de Vries that 
someone else knew about Mendel, so that there would be no point to suppressing a 
knowledge of Mendel's work (if de Vries had, at one time, intended to suppress such 
knowledge). 41 
In the first two rediscovery papers of 1900, de Vries included the maize data (25% 
recessive) in his table of 3:1 F 2 ratios, but said nothing more about xenia. However, 
in his third 1900 rediscovery paper, the longer French paper that appeared in July 
1900, de Vries devoted two pages to a description of his maize experiments because 
they supplied 'a demonstration at the same time very permanent and very easy of 
the law of disjunction of hybrids...'. For the first time de Vries reported extensive F 2 
data for a hybridization experiment. He gave the results of twenty different crosses, 
divided into three groups with respect to kernel colour: for red kernels, 399 
starchy : ll6 sweet; for yellow white kernels, 3161 starchy : 1082 sweet, and for red 
striped kernels, 1239 starchy:387 sweet. In all, there were 4799 starchy: 1585 sweet 
(24"8%). In the Appendix see D.5 of sub-section 6.1. 42 
4. Xenia and ratios before de Vries 
The 1/4:3/4 (sweet to starchy) F 2 ratio reported by de Vries in December 1899 
catches our attention because we know what came in the next year. But in December 
1899, the ratio in itself would not have occasioned any surprise or excited much 
interest. In August 1900 Correns wrote a review of the papers that he and de Vries 
had published on xenia. 'De Vries supported [his view] by an experiment previously 
carried out by KSrnicke, Beal, SturtevanL Crozier, Kellerman and Swingle, Hays 
and probably yet others: he fertilized (1898) a (white) sweet corn, the endosperm of 
which contained dextrin and sugar and wrinkled when dried, with the pollen of a race 
with (white) starch-containing endosperm, and obtained at once kernels, which were 
not to be distinguished from those of the race supplying the pollen. They produced 
(1899) plants with ears, on which 3/4 of the kernels correspond completely to those of 
the ordinary white corn, 1/4 completely to those of sweet corn, a result whic.h 
Kellerman and Swingle, for example, had observed already ...' (my italics). 43 
All those enumerated by Correns, with the exception of KSrnicke, were American 
botanists' of the last quarter of the nineteenth century who had hybridized maize.

Hugo de Vries and the Rediscovery of Mendel's Laws 
529 
Their experiments were summarized by W. Ralph Singleton in an excellent paper 
published over forty years ago and now almost completely fbrgottenff 4 These 
agriculturalists were interested primarily in hybridization as a means of improving 
crop yield, but they also paid attention to the visible effects of hybridization and in 
particular the immediate effect, that is, xenia. Several reported ratios. In 1882, 
W. R. Lazenby crossed sweet corn (r and Flint (starchy) corn (~). The kernels on the 
F 1 ears were Flint. The kernels on the F 2 ears were 'about two-thirds... Flint kernels 
and one-third sweet corn kernels'. In the reciprocal cross, Lazenby found for the F2 
'about one-fifth sweet corn kernels and four-fifths Flint kernels'.45 W. A. Kellerman 
and W. T. Swingle, to whom Correns specifically referred, began experiments in 
1888, three of which deserve mention. For White Flat Ensilage ((r starchy) 
• Landreth Sugar (~, sweet), they noted in the F 2 (1889) that 'the ear consists of 
about one-fourth sweet corn, and the remaining kernels are more or less dented 
[indicating starchy] at summit'. In the cross of Early Landreth Market (~, white 
endosperm) with Early Golden Lenawee (~, yellow endosperm), the F 1 ears 
exhibited xenia (yellow endosperm) and the F 2 ears 'differ[ed] from Early Landreth 
Market in the large proportion (probably 3/4) of yellow kernels...'. In a third cross, 
they found that 'probably seven-eighths of the kernels [of an F2 ear] were Flint'.46 In 
the next year (1890), Kellerman and Swingle found that the sweet F 2 kernels from 
the first cross described above gave rise to plants which, upon self-fertilization, 
yielded 'almost entirely + sweet kernels (F 3), while the starchy kernels produced ears 
which were 'principally white dent, but had intermixed some entirely sweet grains'. 
However, one should not get too excited about these 'Mendelian' results, because 
Kellerman and Swingle regarded them as exceptional and offered no explanation for 
them. Singleton noted that Kellerman actually counted F2 progeny in yet another 
cross (1891) and, although he did not perceive ratios in his data, we can extract 
excellent ones (3 : l, 12 : 3 : l, 9 : 3 : 4)ff 7 
The work of a third corn hybridizer, W. M. Hays, likewise deserves discussion. He 
began experiments in 1888, performing five different sweet • starchy crosses. In the 
F 1 from sweet ~ x starchy ~, Hays reported 'three-fourths like the male parent .... 
while the remaining one-fourth look like sweet grains.' In the F2, Hays observed that 
'while sweet grains produced ears nearly all sweet, the kernels of Flint produced ears 
with about the same proportion of the two kinds as were on the parent ear [F1] 
produced by the cross, viz.: one-fourth Sweet and three-fourths Flint'ff 8 Thus Hays 
discerned a 3:1 F 2 ratio in the progeny of the starchy F 1 plants, but he believed the 
same ratio prevailed in the F 1 . Hays regarded all the kernels on the parent ear as the 
product of hybridization. We realize, just as de Vries realized, that only the starchy 
kernels were hybrids, while the sweet kernels were the result of self-fertilization. But 
Hays thought that maize was self-sterile, so there could not possibly be self-fertilized 
kernels. All kernels had to be true hybrids. Hays's illustration of an F 2 ear
